title: What Spectacles the Legislative Assembly Has Witnessed!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The Tamil section below is a corrected reading copy; the canonical Tamil chapter remains untouched. All eight source pages were checked for the Assembly figures, Cauvery quantities, reported incidents, financial amounts, and closing date. The heavily corrupted list of “Amma” schemes was transcribed directly from source page 158; clearly visible OCR character errors and accidental word breaks were corrected only in the reading copy.
paragraphs: What exactly is happening in Tamil Nadu? Is there such a thing as a Government? If there is, is it functioning?

The Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly session, long spoken of as something that was coming and coming, has now concluded after twenty-nine days, counting from the day of the Governor's address. The Governor spoke on 17 February 2015. The Budget was presented on 25 March 2015. Now, debates on the demands for grants, conducted in name alone for eighteen days without the slightest spirit of freedom, have ended with Ministers replying through recitations of the “Amma” legend.

How many crores of rupees in announcements did the Ministers make through their departments? And a few days after debate on each department had ended, how many announcements concerning that very department did Chief Minister Jayalalithaa read in the Assembly under Rule 110?

Every day, the Chief Minister came to the Assembly solely for this one task. As soon as she entered the House, Question Hour would be postponed so that no one could raise any issue while she was present. She would immediately read a previously prepared statement under Rule 110. The ruling-party members would all thump their desks together. A few people close to the ruling party would promptly praise the announcement in the statement. Finally, the Speaker would praise Jayalalithaa's statement to the skies, crying “Wonderful! Magnificent!”, invoking the “Guinness” record and the “Mahabharata and Ramayana.” No sooner would that end than she would rise and leave as swiftly as she had arrived, caring nothing for the Assembly or any other forum. That was the grand spectacle the country witnessed throughout this Assembly session!

Jayalalithaa remained in the Assembly on only one day, during the debate on the Police Department's demand for grants, for which the Chief Minister is responsible. We are therefore pleased that her health is good enough for her to sit continuously and participate in the House! Yet one cannot help thinking that she deliberately boycotted debates on all the other demands for grants in a manner that showed contempt for the House, democracy and the people's problems.

Adjournment motions and calling-attention motions submitted by the Opposition were not taken up for debate. The Speaker repeatedly said that replies had not arrived from the various departments. The entire session has ended. If replies still have not come from so many departments, can we not understand how, under AIADMK rule, those departments respect the dignity of the Assembly and perform their work?

Of forty-eight adjournment motions submitted, the Government took up not even one. Of three hundred calling-attention motions, only fourteen were taken up, and that too merely for appearance's sake. This shows the priority this Government gives to matters of public importance affecting the people of the State. Yet the Speaker announced in the House, with lavish praise, that the Chief Minister had made 181 announcements under Rule 110. Members who thanked and praised her each time she made one of those announcements praised her again when the total was reported.

Although the Opposition repeatedly asked what had become of the announcements made under Rule 110 in previous years, neither a White Paper nor any reply was provided. From that alone, we can understand their fate.
How many reports have appeared in the newspapers on this one day alone?

A meeting of the Cauvery Monitoring Committee was held in Delhi. There too, like a thunderbolt striking the heads of farmers in the delta districts, the Karnataka Government threw up its hands and said it could not give Tamil Nadu water. Under the Cauvery Tribunal's final award, Karnataka must supply Tamil Nadu 92 TMC of water each year. By the end of August, Karnataka owed us 48 TMC. From October through January 2016, it must supply 47.5 TMC. But Karnataka officials told the meeting that rain had failed there too, drought prevailed, and they were in no position to release water. The talks therefore ended in failure.

In these circumstances, farmers in the Cauvery delta districts, driven to despair by anguish, are conducting shop closures, rail blockades, bus blockades and other protests to save the samba crop cultivated across lakhs of acres.

More than one lakh nutritious-meal employees work across Tamil Nadu. They have been agitating for various demands, including permanent employment, higher wages and the filling of vacancies. When they marched in Chennai and asked to meet the Chief Minister, they were delayed and deceived with the promise that their representatives would be taken to a Minister. When they then attempted a road blockade, the police baton-charged them. But in the Assembly, like the other Ministers, the Minister responsible for this department denied the truth and claimed that no baton charge had occurred.

Members of the National South Indian Rivers Inter-Linking Farmers' Association also staged a half-naked protest on Anna Salai, pressing six demands that included stopping seizure proceedings against farmers and waiving their loans, declaring Tamil Nadu a drought-hit State, and providing proper drought relief to farmers without discrimination.
In Tiruvallur district, 313 people were arrested for staging an occupation protest in the Kosasthalaiyar River, demanding the immediate closure of a newly opened sand quarry.

When the Sri Lankan Navy attacked Tamil Nadu fishermen who had gone fishing, ramming their boat with a vessel, the boat broke apart and sank. No one knows what happened to Vilvaraj, who jumped into the sea. The Sri Lankan Navy captured the other three. It also attacked and chased away fishermen who went in another boat to search for Vilvaraj. Whenever Indian fishermen are arrested by the Sri Lankan Navy, the Tamil Nadu Government considers its work finished as soon as it writes a letter to the Prime Minister.

Following the Tamil Nadu Government's permission to an Adani Group company to establish a solar-power station near Kamuthi in Ramanathapuram district, attempts are being made there through brokers to purchase agricultural land in the surrounding areas cheaply and through irregular means. This angered villagers, and two Adani Group employees suffered sickle injuries and were admitted to hospital.

A three-month-old infant who received a vaccination at the Semmenchery Government Primary Health Centre became ill and died after immediate treatment was not provided. The child's relatives and friends consequently staged a road blockade on Rajiv Gandhi Salai.
Law-college students split into two groups and engaged in a violent clash. In Tondiarpet, three houses were burgled on a single day, and residents surrounded the police station.

One of the statements Chief Minister Jayalalithaa read under Rule 110 on the Assembly's final day announced that an “Amma Literary Award” would be given annually to a woman writer. The Chief Minister even announced that herself. These rulers were unwilling to let anyone else announce even that scheme bearing the “Amma” name.

Under AIADMK rule, they might have stopped after naming one or two schemes after their party leader. But how many schemes bear the name “Amma”? Do they not feel even a little ashamed to name so many? Is the Government their party's private property?

“Amma” Maligai, “Amma” pharmacy, “Amma” water, “Amma” salt, “Amma” cement, “Amma” canteen, “Amma” drinking water, “Amma” Amudham departmental stores, “Amma” seeds, “Amma” baby-care gift kit, and “Amma” full-body check-up: now, in this sequence, an “Amma Literary Award” has been announced in competition with the awards of the Central Institute of Classical Tamil.
An industrial hub costing ₹13,314 crore at Ponneri, they say; it was announced yesterday. Does any of it sound believable?

How many crores of rupees does the Tamil Nadu Government's annual Budget contain? Even yesterday's first supplementary estimates sought and obtained approval for only ₹1,012.30 crore! For how many crores have the demands for grants been approved? What is the financial estimate for the 181 announcements made by the Chief Minister during this term, that is, over four and a half years? From which fund will that amount be allocated? Has the Assembly approved that funding? If approval has not been obtained, are all these schemes destined never to be implemented?
Article 266 of the Constitution of India says that no expenditure may be made without the Legislative Assembly's approval. Does that Article have no meaning at all under AIADMK rule?

On this subject, in the “Kazhugu” answers in this week's *Junior Vikatan*, the question was asked: “When Tamil Nadu is already staggering under a burden of debt, new schemes are being announced every day under Rule 110. Where will the money for all this come from?” The answer given was: “Money is needed only if a scheme is implemented. What is there to worry about if they merely announce that they will announce it?”
The announcements under Rule 110 contain the same blatant fraud practised when announcing the scale of industrial investments.

Amid all this, it is said that an AIADMK Government is functioning. Responding to the Police Department's demand for grants, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa boasted that law and order in Tamil Nadu stood at the very pinnacle. But what has been the condition of law and order over the past ten days?
On 20 September 2015, a notorious rowdy was hacked to death in broad daylight at Guduvancheri.

On 21 September, daily-wage worker Srinivasan was murdered at Thoraipakkam; jewellery was snatched from a woman in T. Nagar; ₹4 lakh was stolen near Ambattur; jewellery was snatched from a woman on a bus travelling from Manali to Chennai; ₹1.5 lakh in wedding-gift money was stolen from a marriage hall in Anna Nagar; a rowdy was hacked to death near the Dindigul court; one hundred sovereigns of jewellery were robbed near Ulundurpet; and coconut trader Rajalingam was murdered near Tiruchendur.
On 22 September, a BJP functionary was murdered in Tirunelveli, and Vijayaganeshan, an office-bearer of a traders' federation, was hacked to death in Maraimalai Nagar.
On 23 September, rowdy Murali was stabbed to death in Royapettah, Chennai, and two robbers were beaten to death near Sriperumbudur.

On 24 September, a temple offertory box was broken open and robbed at an Amman temple in Ennore; Baby Ammal was murdered and her jewellery robbed in West Mambalam; and jewellery was snatched from a woman councillor in Chitlapakkam.

On 25 September, jewellery worth ₹6 crore and ₹17 lakh in cash were robbed in broad daylight from a finance company in Batlagundu; Sarala was beaten to death by her husband in Sholavaram; and a woman railway employee was robbed at knifepoint.

On 26 September, real-estate businessman Babu was murdered near Vandalur; jewellery and cash worth ₹5 lakh were robbed from a teacher's house in Kundrathur; and Bahujan Samaj Party secretary Prabhakaran was hacked to death near Tiruvallur.

On 27 September, a woman's hands and legs were tied and her jewellery robbed in Tondiarpet; thirty sovereigns of jewellery were robbed from a Sub-Inspector's house near Tambaram; thirty sovereigns were stolen from an engineer's house in Madambakkam; and a chain was snatched from a woman during a midnight home invasion in R.K. Nagar, North Chennai.
On 28 September, a woman IT employee was beaten to death, and three houses in Tondiarpet were broken into and robbed.
The list goes on and on.

Seeing all this, people from every section believe that what is functioning in Tamil Nadu is a false Government, a sham Government, a Government that is of no use whatsoever to the public. The Ministers, without the slightest fear, are interested only in how much wealth they can amass during the few days remaining to them. Some officials behave as though it makes no difference who goes and who comes. Some Opposition parties proceed under the fantasy that they will next occupy the seat of power and that one among them will become Chief Minister.

The ruling party's leadership thinks: there is no unity among the Opposition; all we need do is ensure that no one joins with anyone else; then we need only take a portion of the wealth we have amassed; after all, do we not alone possess the power of money and the power of the press?

The wish of the overwhelming majority of Tamils is that the people understand all these crooked manoeuvres, carefully observe and judge who truly works for them and which party's Government would bring good to the State, and come forward to act accordingly. To fulfil that wish without allowing it to be scattered, I lovingly call upon the people of Tamil Nadu: come forward, come forward!
With affection,
M.K.
1 October 2015
